title: Open Gromov-Witten Invariants in Genus Zero
speaker: Amanda Hirschi
abstract: Apart from the usual transversality problems in defining curve counting invariants, when defining the open Gromov-Witten invariants of a Lagrangian one has to deal with the fact that the moduli spaces have boundary. Thus a homological (virtual) fundamental class is usually not available. Assuming regularity, Solomon-Tukachinsky provide a way around this (in genus zero) by first constructing a weakly curved A_infity algebra and then extracting from this enumerative invariants. In this talk I will discuss how their framework can be generalised to deal with general (relatively spin, closed) Lagrangian, using the construction of a global Kuranishi chart for moduli spaces of open stable maps. Our construction can be extended to Lagrangian cobordisms and I will explain how these invariants behave with respect to the relation of being Lagrangian cobordant. Time permitting, I will also discuss some results in higher genus. This is joint work in progress with Kai Hugtenburg.

proof_stages.label: Basic obstructions to open Gromov-Witten counts
proof_stages.summary: The talk motivates open Gromov-Witten theory as counts of holomorphic curves with boundary on a Lagrangian, then isolates the two main obstacles: orientability of the open moduli spaces and their boundary/corner strata, motivating relative spin hypotheses and chain-level rather than homological constructions.

proof_stages.label: Open stable-map moduli spaces and their boundary types
proof_stages.summary: After fixing a tame almost-complex structure, a relatively spin embedded Lagrangian, and relative homology data, the speaker defines moduli spaces of bordered stable maps and explains how boundary nodes, disk bubbling, boundary collapse, and clutching describe their recursive boundary geometry.

proof_stages.label: Global Kuranishi charts, orientations, and evaluation submersions
proof_stages.summary: The first main results assert that the open stable-map moduli spaces admit global Kuranishi charts with boundary/corners, that relative spin data canonically orients them, and that the evaluation maps at interior and boundary marked points can be extended to the thickenings as submersions, with equivalent charts obtained from different auxiliary choices.

proof_stages.label: Boundary recursion and cobordism compatibility for charts
proof_stages.summary: The recursive description of boundary strata is lifted from moduli spaces to the Kuranishi-chart level, where restriction to a boundary stratum is equivalent to a fiber product of charts, and the same chart constructions are extended to embedded Lagrangian cobordisms.

proof_stages.label: Disk-level correspondences and chain operations
proof_stages.summary: Restricting to disks, the speaker uses thickened moduli spaces with one outgoing boundary marked point and interior/boundary incoming marked points to define operations on differential forms by pullback along incoming evaluations, multiplication by Thom representatives of obstruction bundles, and pushforward along the outgoing evaluation.

proof_stages.label: Curved A-infinity structure and auxiliary operations
proof_stages.summary: With compatible choices of Thom forms, the disk operations form a curved A-infinity algebra up to the usual finite-energy/finite-marked-point inductive choices and pseudo-isotopies, while additional operations without outgoing boundary points and with closed curves compensate for boundary-collapse phenomena.

proof_stages.label: Relative potential, open invariants, and invariance statements
proof_stages.summary: The operations define a cycle in a cone complex, a choice of splitting gives a relative potential whose coefficients are the genus-zero open Gromov-Witten invariants, and the cobordism formalism yields invariance under natural deformations while pointing toward higher-genus open-closed field-theoretic structures.

examples.name: Earlier symmetry-protected open invariants
examples.note: The historical cases with anti-symplectic involutions, Maslov-index-zero Lagrangians, or S^1-actions illustrate special settings where open invariants had previously been extracted despite boundary phenomena.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker summarizes prior invariant constructions using anti-symplectic involutions or S^1 actions without carrying out any calculation or construction on them

examples.name: A bounding chain for a null-homologous Lagrangian
examples.note: When L bounds a chain B in X, integrating over B gives a concrete geometric way to choose the splitting needed in the cone construction of the relative potential.
